steps.prompt: Which word is the main clause (apodosis) verb?

hints.interfici: 'interfici' is a passive infinitive meaning 'to be killed' — it complements 'iusserit'
hints.iusserit: 'iusserit' is in the conditional clause (protasis) — not the main clause verb
hints.me: 'me' is a pronoun in the accusative

hints.to sit back: 'to sit back' is close but imprecise — 'residere' means 'to remain/settle/linger'
hints.to reside: 'to reside' is closer to 'habitare' — 'residere' means 'to remain/stay behind'

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'residet' — 'residebit' with -ebit ending is future
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'resideat' — 'residebit' is future indicative
hints.Future perfect indicative: Future perfect would be 'resederit' — 'residebit' is simple future

hints.sedeo, sedere, sedi, sessum: These are the parts of 'sedere' (to sit) — the base verb
hints.resisto, resistere, restiti, —: These are the parts of 'resistere' (to resist) — a different verb

hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect and future perfect look alike ('iusserit'), but in a 'si' clause paired with future main verb, it is future perfect
hints.Perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive is the same form, but the future indicative 'residebit' in the main clause confirms a future condition
hints.Pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'iussisset' — 'iusserit' is future perfect

hints.iungo, iungere, iunxi, iunctum: These are the parts of 'iungere' (to join)
hints.iuro, iurare, iuravi, iuratum: These are the parts of 'iurare' (to swear)
hints.iudico, iudicare, iudicavi, iudicatum: These are the parts of 'iudicare' (to judge)

steps.prompt: What type of conditional is 'si … iusserit … residebit'?
steps.target: Future more vivid — 'if he will have ordered, X will remain'
choices: Future more vivid — 'if he will have ordered, X will remain'

hints.Simple present condition: Simple conditions use present indicative — this uses future perfect + future
hints.Present contrary-to-fact: Contrary-to-fact uses subjunctive — both verbs here are indicative (future system)
hints.Past contrary-to-fact: Past contrary-to-fact uses pluperfect subjunctive — these are indicative

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'ego' — 'me' is accusative, subject of the infinitive 'interfici'
hints.Ablative: 'me' can be ablative, but as subject of the passive infinitive it is accusative

translation: But if he orders me to be killed, [the danger] will remain.
